calculated_metrics.confidence_note: This unit cost is estimated by PRISM by dividing total programme expenditure by the total quantified animal beneficiaries.

details.calculation: ($973,368 USD / 3,054 total beneficiaries) = $318.72 USD per outcome. | A $1,000 USD donation achieves ≈ 3 outcomes.

calculated_metrics.name: Cost Per Outcome (USD)

Subsequently, ACRES filed a case with the Animal and Veterinary Service (AVS), expressing concerns and requesting a joint unannounced inspection.", "resolved_url": "https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1SQaw65x2Ays54qtPn5Vd_CerH6MPBRBw#page=23"}}, {"name": "Undercover Investigations And Exposes", "source": {"source_type": "attached_report", "source_index": 0, "page_number": 24, "search_result_index": null, "quote": "On 18 April 2023, at 11:25 pm, the ACRES hotline received a screen-recorded video from a concerned member of the public. They then dragged the python to a notice board area beside a food stall and repeatedly struck its head with a cleaver until the snake was dead. Following investigations and interviews at the site early the next morning (19 April 2023), ACRES identified police cameras in the area and confirmed a snake sighting around 1:30 am on 18 April 2023. details.formula: Mapping of verified intervention types to the Intervention Leverage Tier framework.

details.elaboration: Quote: 'This financial year, our ACIU (Animal Crime Investigation Unit) investigated a total of 179 cases related to animal cruelty and illegal wildlife trade, in comparison to last FY which saw around 154 cases and over 230 cases in the year before.'
details.formula: Highest level of self-reported evidence cited in impact metrics

details.calculation: A quantified counterfactual baseline was provided.
details.formula: Presence of a quantified counterfactual baseline
Proportional analysis of beneficiary populations (farmed/wild vs. companion)

details.calculation: Organisation self-reported on unintended negative impacts.
details.elaboration: Quote: 'Sadly, not every wild animals whom we rescue survive. Some are so badly injured or suffering from such severe shock that they pass away.'
details.formula: Check for self-reported unintended consequences or failures.

details.calculation: No disclosure of euthanasia or live-release rates found.
details.elaboration: This check rewards transparency for a sensitive operational metric.
details.formula: For direct rescue orgs, check for euthanasia/live-release rate disclosure.

In total, 3,054 wild animals were rescued, including those from the illegal exotic pet trade, often found abandoned in public spaces, as well as native wildlife who were injured, trapped, or displaced.
ACRES is an animal protection organisation, driven by our concern for animals.

metrics.context_qualifier: Total wild animals rescued in Singapore during the financial year, including those from illegal exotic pet trade and native wildlife.

source.quote: In total, 3,054 wild animals were rescued, including those from the illegal exotic pet trade, often found abandoned in public spaces, as well as native wildlife who were injured, trapped, or displaced.

metrics.context_qualifier: Native wild animals successfully released back into their natural habitats during the financial year.

source.quote: More than 1,040 native wild animals were successfully released back into their natural habitats and three exotic wild reptiles were repatriated to the wild this financial year.

metrics.context_qualifier: Individuals reached through outreach programmes and humane education during the financial year.

source.quote: 28,511 Individuals Reached Out To

metrics.context_qualifier: Calls handled by the 24-hour wildlife rescue hotline from members of the public during the financial year.

source.quote: 14,447 Calls to the Hotline

metrics.context_qualifier: Cases investigated by the Animal Crime Investigation Unit related to animal cruelty and illegal wildlife trade.

counterfactual_baseline.description: Last FY saw around 154 cases, indicating an increase in cases handled this year.

source.quote: This financial year, our ACIU (Animal Crime Investigation Unit) investigated a total of 179 cases related to animal cruelty and illegal wildlife trade, in comparison to last FY which saw around 154 cases and over 230 cases in the year before.

metrics.context_qualifier: Cases/emails related to human-wildlife conflicts and interactions handled by the coexistence team.

source.quote: ACRES team also has advised a total of 721 calls/cases/emails related to feedback on human-wildlife conflicts and interactions.

metrics.context_qualifier: Humane education programmes delivered in public and educational spaces during the financial year.

counterfactual_baseline.description: Previous financial year delivered 133 programmes, indicating an increase this year.

source.quote: In this financial year, our humane education team delivered 162 programmes in public and educational spaces, in comparison to 133 and 93 programmes in the previous financial years.

metrics.context_qualifier: Total number of animals rescued from glue traps in Singapore during the financial year.

source.quote: In this financial year, the total number of animals rescued from glue traps in Singapore, by ACRES is 242.

metrics.context_qualifier: Student projects engaged with staff, guided by ACRES, during the financial year.

counterfactual_baseline.description: Previous year engaged with 55 projects, indicating an increase this year.

source.quote: During this financial year, students engaged with us in 67 projects, guided by our staff, in comparison with previous year's 55 projects.

metrics.context_qualifier: Total volunteer hours clocked by supporters during the financial year.

source.quote: 15,313 Volunteer Hours Clocked

metrics.context_qualifier: Reptiles repatriated from the illegal wildlife trade during the financial year.

source.quote: ACRES repatriated 3 reptiles from the illegal wildlife trade, in this financial year, on 18th July 2023, scoring another victory for wildlife against the cruel and wasteful illegal wildlife trade.

significant_events.event_name: Intervention against live crocodile handling event

source.quote: On 17 May 2023, the ACRES Animal Crime Investigation Unit (ACIU) was alerted to an event organized by the Kranji Countryside Association, which advertised the handling of live crocodiles. In response, ACIU wrote to the Animal and Veterinary Service (AVS), citing research on the stress this would cause to the animals and highlighting the lack of educational value in the activity. AVS responded by confirming that the event would no longer feature live crocodiles, opting instead to use taxidermy specimens.

significant_events.summary: ACIU intervened against an event advertising live crocodile handling, leading to the organizer opting for taxidermy specimens instead.

significant_events.event_name: Apprehension and prosecution of pangolin seller

source.quote: On 5 July 2023, the ACRES Animal Crime Investigation Unit (ACIU) received a report about an individual attempting to sell a live pangolin on Telegram. ACIU raised concerns that the pangolin might have been wild-caught in Singapore and filed a case with the NParks for investigation. The individual was apprehended by NParks and later prosecuted in court, as reported in media.

significant_events.summary: ACIU reported an individual attempting to sell a live pangolin on Telegram, leading to apprehension and prosecution by NParks.

significant_events.event_name: Investigation and case filing for welfare of horses and ponies

source.quote: On 8 June 2023, the ACRES Animal Crime Investigation Unit (ACIU) received a report from a concerned member of the public regarding the welfare of horses and ponies at Gallop Stable (Turf City). An Instagram page had been created by someone to raise awareness and shared images of the actual conditions and overall welfare standards of the animals. ACIU contacted individuals who provided feedback to verify the allegations. Subsequently, ACRES filed a case with the Animal and Veterinary Service (AVS), expressing concerns and requesting a joint unannounced inspection.

significant_events.summary: ACIU received a report regarding the welfare of horses and ponies at Gallop Stable, leading to contact with individuals and filing a case with AVS.

significant_events.event_name: Investigation and fine for attacking protected reticulated python

source.quote: On 18 April 2023, at 11:25 pm, the ACRES hotline received a screen-recorded video from a concerned member of the public. The video showed a man and five others violently attacking a protected reticulated python at the bicycle stand of Boon Lay Place market, using crates. They then dragged the python to a notice board area beside a food stall and repeatedly struck its head with a cleaver until the snake was dead. Following investigations and interviews at the site early the next morning (19 April 2023), ACRES identified police cameras in the area and confirmed a snake sighting around 1:30 am on 18 April 2023. The ACRES Animal Crime Investigation Unit (ACIU) submitted all relevant information to NParks, who investigated the case. The man involved was fined $1,000 for the incident.

significant_events.summary: ACIU investigated a video of individuals attacking a protected reticulated python, leading to NParks investigation and a $1,000 fine for the perpetrator.

significant_events.event_name: Stakeholder engagement on glue trap regulation

source.quote: Aligned with the findings, ACRES organised a stakeholder engagement on 29th February 2024 with the pest control professionals, SPMA (Singapore Pest Management Association), Mandai Nature and the SPCA. This closed door discussion resulted in a positive consensus that sale and use of glue traps by public and to public should be regulated, and there are no guidelines on how a trapped rodent should be humanely killed.

significant_events.summary: ACRES organized a stakeholder engagement with pest control professionals and other organizations to discuss glue trap regulation.

significant_events.event_name: Investigation into online illegal wildlife trade on Telegram

source.quote: ACRES addressed our growing concerns on the rapid surge in online illegal wildlife trade, through an investigation into wildlife trade on Telegram in Singapore.

significant_events.summary: ACRES conducted an investigation into the rapid surge of online illegal wildlife trade on Telegram in Singapore.

significant_events.event_name: Repatriation of 3 reptiles from illegal wildlife trade

source.quote: ACRES repatriated 3 reptiles from the illegal wildlife trade, in this financial year, on 18th July 2023, scoring another victory for wildlife against the cruel and wasteful illegal wildlife trade.

significant_events.summary: ACRES successfully repatriated 3 reptiles from the illegal wildlife trade on 18th July 2023.

significant_events.event_name: Rescue, rehabilitation, and release of plantain squirrel

source.quote: On 27 January 2024, the ACRES rescue hotline received a call about a plantain squirrel being kept in a cage at an industrial estate. The squirrel was confined in a dirty metal cage, surrounded by old food that had been left for days. ACIU engaged the owner to understand, who then explained that the squirrel had been picked up as a young animal and hand-raised, primarily fed with vegetables, fruits, and red bean baos. The squirrel was surrendered to ACRES in February 2024 for rehabilitation and eventual release in April 2024.

significant_events.summary: A plantain squirrel kept illegally in a cage was rescued, rehabilitated, and released back to the wild.

source.quote: Singapore's first-ever “Coexistence Handbook” was launched to the public, offering comprehensive guidance on wildlife encounters, including detailed exclusion measures and insights into wildlife behaviour.

significant_events.summary: ACRES launched a comprehensive handbook offering guidance on wildlife encounters and coexistence with native wildlife.

source.quote: In April 2022, Singapore's Urban Wildlife Working Group members together with other partners in the nature community launched the Our Wild Neighbours (OWN) initiative - a consolidated, country-wide outreach programme to promote coexistence with Singapore's native wildlife.

significant_events.summary: A country-wide outreach program launched in April 2022 to promote coexistence with Singapore's native wildlife, continuing into the current FY.

source.quote: In this financial year, our humane education team delivered 162 programmes in public and educational spaces, in comparison to 133 and 93 programmes in the previous financial years.

significant_events.summary: The humane education team delivered 162 programmes in public and educational spaces during the financial year.

source.quote: During this financial year, students engaged with us in 67 projects, guided by our staff, in comparison with previous year's 55 projects.

significant_events.summary: ACRES staff engaged with students in 67 projects, guiding them in animal welfare advocacy.

significant_events.event_name: Participation in Wild Animal Rescue Network conference

source.quote: In June 2023, ACRES Co-CEO Kalai presented at the Wild Animal Rescue Network conference, held in Hua Hin, Thailand. The conference was attended by 2 of our rescue officers to learn from fellow wildlife advocates, on rescue and rehabilitation programmes.

significant_events.summary: ACRES Co-CEO and rescue officers attended the Wild Animal Rescue Network conference to learn about rescue and rehabilitation.

significant_events.event_name: Presentation at Asia Farm Animal Day conference

source.quote: In October 2023, ACRES Co-CEO Anbu presented at the Asia Farm Animal Day conference in Kuching, on farm animal welfare work and campaigns.

significant_events.summary: ACRES Co-CEO presented on farm animal welfare work and campaigns at the Asia Farm Animal Day conference.

significant_events.event_name: Presentation at Asia For Animals online conference

source.quote: In October 2023, ACRES Co-CEO Anbu presented at the Asia For Animals online conference in Kuching, Sarawak. She also was a panellist on a discussion on online animal cruelty content. Two rescue officers and 3 advocacy members joined Anbu to attend and learn from other organisations around the world.

significant_events.summary: ACRES Co-CEO presented and participated in a panel discussion on online animal cruelty content at the Asia For Animals online conference.

source.quote: In this financial year, we carried out extensive renovation works to improve the quality of space at our bird rehabilitation block.

significant_events.summary: Extensive renovation works were carried out to improve the quality and capacity of the bird rehabilitation block.

source.quote: Sadly, not every wild animals whom we rescue survive. Some are so badly injured or suffering from such severe shock that they pass away.
Sadly, not every wild animals whom we rescue survive. Some are so badly injured or suffering from such severe shock that they pass away.

data.original_text: Our talks, roadshows and Humane Education programmes touch a wide cross section of society, from children in kindergarten to the elderly in the heartland, reaching close to 50,000 individuals a (non-Covid) year.
They currently have over 20,000 volunteers and supporters in their database.

In the 2019 financial year (ending 31st March 2019), our amazing volunteers clocked over 18,300 hours; our education team were directly involved with 72 student projects, and reached out to over 42,000 individuals, through over 140 roadshows and talks; our rescue team handled 9,811 calls to our hotline, leading to rescue of over 3,300 wild animals in distress.
It aims to raise awareness of animal welfare issues, adopts animal research projects and their findings for public outreach and education.
Our sole aim is to advocate for an end to animal cruelty in Asia.
ACRES gets approval to use its original land that was previously contaminated by the contractor.
ACRES rescues, rehabilitates and releases Oil-spilled kingfisher; On 14 June 2024, an oil spill in Sentosa made an impact on Singapore's wildlife.
Landmark repatriation of 51 Indian star tortoises back to the wild in India, along with partner NGO Wildlife SOS in India.
ACRES opens additional outdoor sanctuary to make room for tortoises to continue experiencing outdoor environment.
Innovative community wildlife rescue team is launched, with PDF advisories to seek help from public to help distressed wildlife upon our assessment and advise.
ACRES repatriates another 6 reptiles back to the wild in Malaysia: four giant Asian pond turtles and two elongated tortoises.
ACRES launches our 24-Hour Wildlife Crime Hotline (97837782), for members of the public to blow the whistle on those illegally trading in or owning wild animals and their parts.
Since its launch, hundreds of wild animals have been rescued by ACRES, and several individuals illegally trading in wild animals and wild animal products have been brought to task.
A 6-month undercover investigation exposed over 150 prohibited wildlife being sold online in Singapore.
ACRES repatriates her first primate to Malaysia from the illegal wildlife trade in Singapore – a pig-tailed macaque.
ACRES opens a brand new aviary for rehabilitation of rescued native species of birds before they can be returned to the wild.

Their first-ever reptile gets repatriated back to the wild, after being rescued from the illegal wildlife trade: Rahayu, a Malaysian giant turtle, an endangered species of freshwater turtle who was found on Singapore's roads with fish hook and line in her mouth.

2017 sees increase in the number of rescue calls totaling to over 27,000 calls (since 2010) to our hotline related to wildlife in distress; rescued over 14,000 wild animals and released over 7,000 wild animals back to the wild.
Due to intensive joint efforts from NParks and Otterwatch, ACRES cares for her first otter pup rescued and reunites with the family.

First time repatriation of two Asian Leopard cats confiscated as a result of ACRES's investigations in 2018, due to joint efforts from National Parks Board, Wildlife Reserves of Singapore and the Malaysian Wildlife Department (Perhilitan).
Two turtles that had been caught in Singapore's illegal pet trade were successfully repatriated.
In the 2019 financial year (ending 31st March 2019)... our rescue team handled 9,811 calls to our hotline, leading to rescue of over 3,300 wild animals in distress.

Our Animal Crime Investigation Unit (ACIU) handled over 230 cases related to wildlife trade and cruelty and the wildlife management team engaged community through over 100 cases of human-wildlife conflict in Singapore.
Ending the sale of live soft-shelled turtles, frogs and eels at Singapore's wet markets was another landmark achievement.
Despite the challenges, 2021 saw a 65% increase in the number of calls handled by ACRES's 24-hour team, which resulted in several upgrades to our current rehabilitation capabilities.
Significant drops in illegal wildlife trade levels in Singapore have been recorded in recent years.
For example, our undercover investigations in 2001 revealed that 73.5% of TCM shops in Singapore were selling bear products; by 2005 this figure had dropped to 20%.
Our investigations have led to the capture of many wildlife criminals.
Our campaigns and engagements with the government have led to vast improvements to national wildlife protection laws.
We have rescued and relocated more than 10000 injured or displaced animals to date.
We have also conducted over 2700 outreach programmes in the past...
ACRES received the Municipal Services Award for their efforts in managing and implementing the Otter-Proofing for Coexistence project in Seletar Hills Estate.

Singapore's first-ever “Coexistence Handbook” was launched to the public, offering comprehensive guidance on wildlife encounters, including detailed exclusion measures and insights into wildlife behaviour.

name: Animal Concerns Research and Education Society
